title: Individual Income Tax Return Statistics (2024 tax year) - Canada.ca
content:
## Individual Income Tax Return Statistics (2024 tax year)
Individual Income Tax Return Statistics presents data from individual income tax and benefit returns that were processed for the 2024 tax year. These statistics contain the most recent 2024 tax year assessments or reassessments up to the cut-off date of November 30, 2025.
- Explanatory notes Confidentiality procedures
- Confidentiality procedures
- Major changes for the 2024 tax year
- Major classification variables Taxable or non-taxable classification Income ranges classification Source of income classification Age and gender classification Provincial or territorial classification
- Taxable or non-taxable classification
- Income ranges classification
- Source of income classification
- Age and gender classification
- Provincial or territorial classification
- Description of items Number of returns Income items Deduction items Non-refundable tax credits Tax payable
- Non-refundable tax credits
- Description of tables
- Tables in PDF format
- Tables in CSV format
The 2026 edition of the Individual Income Tax Return Statistics presents data based on 2024 tax year returns filed and processed up to November 30, 2025. The most recent assessment is used to compile the statistics. As a result, for returns that have been reassessed, the most current reassessed values are used to prepare the tables.
All of the statistics in this publication are subject to revision.

### Confidentiality procedures
To ensure the protection of tax filer information, data have been suppressed where warranted. As well, counts are rounded to the nearest multiple of 10. For example, 104 would be rounded to 100 and 105 would be rounded to 110. Dollar amounts have been rounded to the nearest thousand in Tables 1 to 5. Totals may not add up due to rounding or suppression. A zero [0] indicates that the information has been suppressed for confidentiality purposes. Suppressed information also includes valid zeroes.

## Major changes for the 2024 tax year
The major changes for the 2024 tax year are listed below. For more details on these and other changes, see the areas outlined in the Federal Income Tax and Benefit Guide – 2024.
CPP/QPP Contributions - A new additional CPP/QPP contribution has been introduced. The maximum additional contribution for 2024 is $838.00, compared to $631.00 in 2023. (Item 40)
Volunteer firefighters’ amount/search and rescue volunteers’ amount - The employment amount for volunteer firefighters and the search and rescue volunteer amount have doubled. Eligible individuals with at least 200 hours of volunteer service in 2024 may now claim $6,000, up from the previous $3,000. (Item 72)
Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) - Significant amendments have been made to the AMT for 2024. These include a higher AMT rate and exemption amount, a revised calculation of taxable income for AMT purposes, updated treatment of the foreign tax credit, and new limits on the use of certain non‑refundable tax credits. (Item 97)

## Major classification variables
The following variables are used in one or more of the tables in this publication:
- Taxable or non-taxable classification
- Income ranges classification
- Source of income classification
- Age and gender classification
- Provincial or territorial classification

### Taxable or non-taxable classification
A tax return is considered taxable when the sum of net federal tax, net provincial tax, Canada Pension Plan (CPP) contributions payable on self-employment earnings, employment insurance (EI) premiums payable on self-employment earnings, and social benefit repayment amounts is at least $2. It is non-taxable when this sum is less than $2.
Some returns are classified as taxable even when the return showed a total income less than the allowable basic personal amount. This happened for:
- individuals subject to the alternative minimum tax
- returns filed by certain non-residents for income earned in Canada that is not subject to personal amounts
- individuals who resided in Canada for only part of a tax year (these individuals paid tax on the income they earned during their period of residence in Canada, and as a result, they are eligible for tax credits only for that period)
- individuals who had CPP contributions or EI premiums payable on self-employment earnings
- individuals who had to repay social benefits
It is possible for individuals classified in the upper income ranges to use a variety of deductions and tax credits to achieve a non-taxable status. Among the deductions they can use are: carrying charges (such as interest paid to earn investment income); business or farm losses of previous years; and allowable business investment losses. Tax filers can also use non-refundable tax credits (such as charitable donations, gifts to Canada or a province or territory, or dividend and foreign tax credits) to reduce their tax to zero.

### Income ranges classification
The income ranges presented in the tables are based on the total income assessed. This corresponds to line 15000 of the return and includes:
- self-employment income
- income from other sources
Total income assessed may differ from the true economic income presented in other publications because it does not include certain non-taxable income. It may, however, include grossed-up income such as income from eligible dividends, which is the value plus 38%.
See the detailed list of other forms of non-taxable incomes in the description for total income assessed.

### Source of income classification
Tax filers do not report their type of work or occupation. These classification statistics are based on the  source of income of a tax filer.
For example, if a tax filer earned an employment income as well as investment income, this tax filer will be counted in both categories of employment and investment income.
The following lists nine income classifications the Canada Revenue Agency uses and describes the tax filers who make up each of them:
- Employment income – tax filers employed by a business, institution, school, federal or provincial Crown corporation, or a government body
- Farming and fishing income – self-employed tax filers who earned their source of income from farming or fishing
- Professional income – self-employed tax filers whose source of income is professional fees (including accountants, doctors and surgeons, dentists, lawyers and notaries, engineers and architects, as well as entertainers, artists, etc.) The number of professionals shown in the publication may differ from the number shown in professional directories
- Commissions income (Self-employed) – tax filers whose source of earnings is commission income from self-employment
- Business income – tax filers whose source of income is business income from self-employment
- Investment income – tax filers whose source of income is interest, taxable dividends from Canadian corporations, taxable capital gains, and other investment income
- Pension income – tax filers whose source of income is pension or split pension income
- Social benefits income – tax filers whose source of income is employment insurance, social assistance payments, universal child care benefits (UCCB), workers’ compensation benefits, and net federal supplements
- Other income – tax filers whose source of income is alimony, taxable amount of scholarships, fellowships, bursaries, and artists' project grants, registered retirement savings plan (RRSP) income, registered disability savings plan income, taxable first home savings account (FHSA) income or other unspecified income.

### Age and gender classification
The age of the tax filer is determined from the reported year of birth on page 1 of the T1 Income Tax and Benefit Return.
Gender is categorized as female, male, gender diverse, or unknown. Individuals who identify as non-binary or with any other gender identity that does not fall within the female or male categories are represented in the gender diverse category. Tax filers for whom no gender code is available, for example, non-residents who applied for an Individual Tax Number, are counted in the “unknown” category. In this publication in order to maintain confidentiality, information is presented for female and male category only.

### Provincial or territorial classification
Province or territory of taxation, used in Table 1, refers to the province or territory where provincial or territorial tax is payable.
Province or territory of residence, used in Tables 2 to 4, refers to the province or territory in which the tax filer resided on December 31, 2024, as indicated on their individual income tax and benefit return.

## Description of items
- Non-refundable tax credits

#### Item 1: Number of taxable returns
This is the number of taxable returns as outlined in the Taxable or non-taxable classification description.

#### Item 2: Number of non-taxable returns
This is the number of non-taxable returns as outlined in the Taxable or non-taxable classification description.

#### Item 3: Total number of returns
This is the total number of returns.

#### Item 4: Employment income (before deductions) – Line 10100 of the return, less commissions on line 10120
This is income from wages and salaries, taxable allowances and benefits, bonuses and directors’ fees, etc.

#### Item 5: Commissions – Line 10120 of the return
This is the income an employee received based on a percentage of sales. Some people may be paid by commission only, while others may receive a fixed salary as well as a percentage of sales.

#### Item 6: Other employment income – Line 10400 of the return
This includes the following amounts:
- tips and occasional earnings that may not be included on the information slip
- medical premium benefits
- Wage Earner Protection Program
- Bankruptcy settlement
- employment income not reported on a T4 slip
- clergy’s housing allowance or an amount for eligible utilities
- foreign employment income
- income-maintenance insurance plans (wage-loss replacement plans)
- certain goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax (GST/HST) and Quebec sales tax rebates
- amounts received under a supplementary unemployment benefit plan (a guaranteed annual wage plan)
- taxable benefits for premiums paid for a group term life-insurance plan
- employees profit-sharing plan amounts (EPSP)

#### Item 7: Old Age Security (OAS) pension – Line 11300 of the return
This is a monthly benefit paid to people 65 years of age or older. It is from box 18 of the T4A(OAS) slip.

#### Item 8: CPP or QPP benefits – Line 11400 of the return
This is the Canada Pension Plan (CPP) or Quebec Pension Plan (QPP) benefits, including survivor, disability, child, and death benefits, as outlined on box 20 of the T4A(P) slip. These benefits may be received by a surviving spouse, common-law partner, dependent child, or estate of a deceased contributor, depending on the situation

#### Item 9: Other pensions and superannuation – Line 11500 of the return
This includes income from registered pension plans, registered retirement income funds, deferred profit-sharing plans, foreign pensions, etc.

#### Item 10: Elected split-pension amount – Line 11600 of the return
This is the amount from line 22 of Form T1032, Joint Election to Split Pension Income.
The transferring spouse or common-law partner must deduct on line 21000 of their return the elected split-pension amount from line 22 of Form T1032.

### Income from other sources

#### Item 11: Employment insurance and other benefits – Line 11900 of the return
This refers to benefits received under the Employment insurance plan (box 14 on the T4E slip).

#### Item 12: Taxable amount of dividends (eligible and other than eligible) from taxable Canadian corporations – Line 12000 of the return
This is the total dividend value, plus a 38% gross-up for the eligible dividends and a 15% gross-up for the dividends other than eligible dividends. A dividend tax credit is also available.

#### Item 13: Interest and other investment income – Line 12100 of the return
This includes interest, foreign interest or dividend income.

#### Item 14: Net partnership income: limited or non-active partners only – Line 12200 of the return
This is the share of the net business income or loss from limited partnerships.

#### Item 15: Net rental income – Line 12600 of the return
This is rental income after expenses.

#### Item 16: Taxable capital gains – Line 12700 of the return
This is 50% of the capital gains realized in 2024.

#### Item 17: RRSP income – Line 12900 of the return
This is income from a registered retirement savings plan.

#### Item 18: Other income – Line 13000 of the return
This includes taxable income not reported elsewhere on the return. For more details, see the Federal Income Tax and Benefit Guide – 2024.

#### Item 19: Taxable scholarships, fellowships, bursaries, and artists' project grants – Line 13010 of the return
This is the taxable amount for scholarships, fellowships, bursaries, and artists' project grants.

### Income from self-employment
Self-employment income corresponds to net income. In other words, it is gross income, less any adjustments and expenses incurred.

#### Item 20: Net business income – Line 13500 of the return
This is the income from businesses and active partnerships.

#### Item 21: Net professional income – Line 13700 of the return
This is income from an independent practice only, such as earnings by self-employed accountants, doctors, dentists and lawyers. However, when a professionally qualified person is employed by a company, government or institution, this individual’s income is included in Item 4: Employment income (before deductions).

#### Item 22: Net commission income – Line 13900 of the return
This is net commission income for self-employed people, such as real estate agents, who are working in sales and earning commissions.

#### Item 23: Net farming income – Line 14100 of the return
This is the income of self-employed farmers, including beekeepers and tree farmers.

#### Item 24: Net fishing income – Line 14300 of the return
This is the net income of self-employed people fishing as boat owners or crew members, or fishing from shore.

#### Item 25: Workers’ compensation benefits – Line 14400 of the return
This is the amount of compensation paid to an employee or surviving spouse or common-law partner in the event of the employee’s injury, disability or death, under the laws of Canada or a province or territory. This amount is shown on a T5007, Statement of Benefits slip.

#### Item 26: Social assistance payments – Line 14500 of the return
This is the payments to beneficiaries or third parties as reported on a T5007, Statement of Benefits slip.

#### Item 27: Net federal supplement – Line 14600 of the return
This is the net amount of any allowance, allowance for the survivor, or guaranteed income supplement received in the tax year. It is shown in box 21 of the T4A(OAS) slip.

### Total income assessed

#### Item 28: Total income assessed – Line 15000 of the return
This includes the amount reported on line 15000 of the return. It does not include non-taxable income from the following sources:
- GST/HST credit or Canada child benefit payments, as well as payments from related provincial or territorial programs;
- child assistance payments and the supplement for children with disabilities paid by the province of Quebec;
- amounts received under the Canada child benefit (CCB) program;
- compensation received from a province or territory for a victim of a criminal act or a motor vehicle accident;
- most gifts and inheritances;
- amounts paid by Canada or an ally (if the amount is not taxable in that country) for disability or death due to war service;
- most amounts received from a life insurance policy after someone’s death;
- most payments of the type commonly referred to as strike pay received from a union;
- most amounts received from a tax-free savings account (TFSA).
The income earned on any of the above amounts (such as interest earned from the investment of lottery winnings) is taxable.
Some parts of total income assessed are in gross amounts, while others are in net amounts. For example, eligible dividend income is grossed up to represent 138% of such income. Interest and investment income are also gross figures since carrying charges are not deducted. On the other hand, taxable capital gains are net amounts because only 50% of the gains realized in 2024 are reported.

### Deductions from total income assessed

#### Item 29: Registered pension plan (RPP) deduction – Line 20700 of the return
This is the amount deducted from salaries for registered pension plans.

#### Item 30: RRSP/PRPP deduction - Line 20800 of the return
This is the amount deducted for registered retirement savings plans or pooled registered pension plans. It is reported on line 20800 of the return. The reported amount includes eligible RRSP transfers.

#### Item 31: First home savings account (FHSA) deduction - line 20805 of the return
The First Home Savings Account (FHSA) is a new registered plan designed to help individuals save for their first home. Contributions to an FHSA are generally deductible.

#### Item 32: Deduction for elected split-pension amount – Line 21000 of the return
This is the pension amount that spouses or common-law partners have jointly elected to split (line 22 of Form T1032).

#### Item 33: Annual union, professional or like dues – Line 21200 of the return
- annual dues for membership in a trade union or an association of public servants
- professional board dues required under provincial or territorial law
- professional or malpractice liability insurance premiums or professional membership dues required to keep a professional status recognized by law
- parity or advisory committee (or similar body) dues required under provincial or territorial law
This does not include initiation fees and special assessments, licenses or charges for any purpose other than the organization’s ordinary operating costs.

#### Item 34: Child care expenses – Line 21400 of the return
This is the child care expenses reported on line 21400 of the return.

#### Item 35: Business investment loss – Line 21700 of the return
This is the allowable business investment loss in 2024. Refer to Guide T4037, Capital Gains.

#### Item 36: Moving expenses – Line 21900 of the return
This is moving expenses paid in 2024 for individuals who moved to get employment, run a business, or study full time at an educational institution offering post-secondary courses—the move must have brought the tax filer at least 40 kilometres closer to their new work or school.

#### Item 37: Support payments made – Line 22000 of the return
This is the deductible amount only of support payments for a spouse or common-law partner or for a child in 2024.

#### Item 38: Carrying charges and interest expenses – Line 22100 of the return or according to the Schedule 4 calculation
The following carrying charges and interest paid to earn income from investments can be claimed:
- fees to manage or take care of investments (other than administration fees paid for a registered retirement savings plan or registered retirement income fund), including safety deposit box charges
- fees for certain investment advice or for recording investment income
- fees paid to have someone complete a return, but only if the tax filer has income from a business or property, accounting is a usual part of the operations of their business or property, and the tax filer did not use the amounts claimed to reduce the business or property income reported;
- most interest paid on money borrowed for investment purposes, but generally only as long as it is used to try to earn investment income, including interest and dividends. However, if the only earnings the investment can produce are capital gains, the interest paid cannot be claimed.

#### Item 39: Deduction for CPP or QPP contributions on self-employment and other earnings - Line 22200 of the return
This item pertains to claiming a deduction for contributions made to the Canada Pension Plan (CPP) or the Quebec Pension Plan (QPP) on self-employment income and other eligible earnings.

#### Item 40: Deduction for CPP or QPP enhanced contributions on employment income - Line 22215 of the return
For 2024 tax year, a deduction can be claimed for the enhanced contributions on CPP and QPP pensionable earnings contributed through employment income. The maximum allowable claim is $838.

#### Item 41: Deduction for provincial parental insurance plan (PPIP) premiums on self-employment income – Line 22300 of the return
This is the PPIP premiums paid by residents of Quebec with a net self-employment income on lines 13499 to 14300 of $2,000 or more or total employment income (including employment income from outside Canada) and net self-employment income of $2,000 or more.

#### Item 42: Exploration and development expenses – Line 22400 of the return
This is deductions for investments in a petroleum, natural gas, or mining venture in 2024, without active participation.

#### Item 43: Other employment expenses – Line 22900 of the return
This includes deductions for certain expenses the tax filer incurred to earn employment income.

#### Item 44: Clergy residence deduction – Line 23100 of the return
This is a calculated deduction available to a member of the clergy for their residence. See Form T1223, Clergy Residence Deduction, for more information.

#### Item 45: Other deductions – Line 23200 of the return
This item includes all other deductions for which no specific line is provided in the return. For more information, consult the Federal Income Tax and Benefit Guide –2024.

#### Item 46: Total deductions before adjustment – Line 23300 of the return

#### Item 47: Social benefits repayment – Line 23500 of the return
This is the amount of employment insurance premiums to be repaid in whole or in part if certain conditions are met. For more information on these conditions, please see the Federal Income Tax and Benefit Guide.
Old Age Security pension (line 11300) or net federal supplements (line 14600) have to be repaid in whole or in part when the net income before adjustments (line 23400) minus lines 11700 and 12500, plus any deduction on line 21300 and/or for a repayment of registered disability savings plans income included on line 23200, is more than $90,997.

#### Item 48: Net income – Line 23600 of the return
This is the result of subtracting the total deductions and social benefits repayment from total income assessed.

### Deductions from net income

#### Item 49: Canadian Forces personnel and police deduction – Line 24400 of the return
This is the deduction for certain members of the Canadian Forces and Canadian police services deployed outside Canada on a high-risk or current moderate-risk operational mission.

#### Item 50: Security options deductions – Line 24900 of the return
This item corresponds to the total of the amounts entered in boxes 39 and 41 of the T4 for transactions carried out no later than June 24, 2024, or in boxes 91 and 92 for those carried out after that date. If the filer has deferred stock option benefits, they must complete Form T1212 and claim the applicable deduction on line 24900, as well as, if applicable, the additional deduction on line 24901. For more details, consult the Federal Income Tax and Benefit Guide – 2024.

#### Item 51: Other payments deduction – Line 25000 of the return
Generally, the tax filer can deduct the total amount from line 14700 of their return. This includes:
- line 14400, Workers’ compensation benefits;
- line 14500, Social assistance payments;
- line 14600, Net federal supplements.

#### Item 52: Non-capital losses of other years – Line 25200 of the return
This is the unapplied non-capital losses reported on previous tax years returns and claimed in 2024. For more information, see Line 25200 – Non-capital losses of other years .

#### Item 53: Net capital losses of other years – Line 25300 of the return
This is the net capital losses of previous years that have not already been claimed, within certain limits. For more information, see Guide T4037, Capital Gains.

#### Item 54: Capital gains deduction – Line 25400 of the return
This is a deduction the tax filer may be able to claim for gains realized on the disposition of qualified small business corporation shares, qualified farm property, and qualified fishing property. For more information, see Guide T4037, Capital Gains.

#### Item 55: Northern residents deductions – Line 25500 of the return
This is the total northern residents deductions, which is the sum of the residency deduction for having lived in a prescribed zone and the travel deduction, as calculated on Form T2222, Northern Residents Deductions.

#### Item 56: Additional deductions – Line 25600 of the return
- Income exempt under a tax treaty, for example, foreign income such as support payments received from a resident of another country reported on line 12800
- Earned income and pension benefits given to a religious order by members who have taken a vow of perpetual poverty
- Net employment income reported by employees of prescribed international organizations, such as the United Nations
- Adult basic education tuition assistance shown in box 21 of slip T4E

#### Item 57: Farming and fishing losses of prior years
This is the amount of farming and fishing losses reported on previous tax years returns and claimed in 2024. It also includes the amount of a farm loss subject to the section 31 restrictions. For more information, see Line 25200 – Non-capital losses of other years .

#### Item 58: Total deductions (from net income) – Line 25700 of the return

#### Item 59: Taxable income assessed – Line 26000 of the return
$111,733 to $173,205
$173,205 to $246,752

### Non-refundable tax credits
Tax filers can claim certain non-refundable tax credits in calculating taxes payable for a tax year. These credits reduce the amount of income tax a tax filer owes. If the total of these credits is more than the income tax the tax filer would otherwise owe for the year, the tax filer will not receive a refund for the difference.

#### Item 60: Basic personal amount – Line 30000 of the return
For 2024, this amount is $15,000.

#### Item 61: Age amount – Line 30100 of the return
Tax filers who were 65 years or older in 2024 and whose net income was less than $102,925 may be allowed to claim an age amount up to a maximum of $8,790.

#### Item 62: Spouse or common-law partner amount - Line 30300 of the return
This amount can be claimed by a tax filer who supported their spouse or common-law partner at any time in the year and their spouse's net income was less than the basic personal amount of the tax filer (plus $2,616 if the spouse or common-law partner was dependent on them because of an impairment in physical or mental functions).

#### Item 63: Canada Caregiver Amount - Lines 30425 and 30450 of the return
Caregiver amounts for spouse or common-law partner, or eligible dependant or other infirm dependants aged 18 or older.

#### Item 64: Amount for an eligible dependant – Line 30400 of the return
This is an amount the tax filer may be able to claim if, at any time in the year, they met all of the following conditions at the same time:
- They did not have a spouse or common-law partner or if they did, they were not living with, supporting, or being supported by that person
- They supported a dependant in 2024; and they lived with the dependant (in most cases in Canada) in a home that they maintained—they cannot claim this amount for a person who was only visiting them and, at the time they met the above conditions, the dependant also must have been either: their parent or grandparent by blood, marriage, common-law partnership, or adoption their child, grandchild, brother, or sister by blood, marriage, common-law partnership, or adoption and either under 18 years of age or mentally or physically impaired
- their parent or grandparent by blood, marriage, common-law partnership, or adoption
- their child, grandchild, brother, or sister by blood, marriage, common-law partnership, or adoption and either under 18 years of age or mentally or physically impaired
If the tax filer has a dependant with an impairment in physical or mental functions, the tax filer may be eligible to claim an additional amount of $2,616 on this line.

#### Item 65: Canada caregiver amount for infirm children under 18 years of age– Line 30500 of the return
This is an amount that tax filers can claim for each of their children (or their spouse’s or common-law partner’s children) who are under 18 years of age at the end of the year and have an impairment in physical or mental functions. For 2024, the amount is $2,616.

#### Item 66: CPP or QPP contributions through employment – Line 30800 of the return
CPP and QPP contributions consist of a base amount, a first additional amount, and, beginning in 2024, a second additional amount (CPP2/QPP2) on earnings above the Year’s Maximum Pensionable Earnings  (YMPE) and up to the Year’s Additional Maximum Pensionable Earnings  (YAMPE). These amounts are reported on T4 slips in boxes 16, 16A, 17, and 17A.
Line 30800 includes only the base CPP or QPP contributions that qualify for the federal non‑refundable tax credit.
The first and second additional CPP/QPP contributions are not included on Line 30800 and are instead claimed as a deduction on Line 22215, subject to the annual maximum.
Individuals aged 71 or older are excluded, as they do not contribute.

#### Item 67: Base CPP or QPP contributions on self-employment and other earnings – Line 31000 of the return
This is for the total base Canada Pension Plan (CPP) and Quebec Pension Plan (QPP) contributions calculated on self-employment and other earnings.

#### Item 68: Employment insurance premiums through employment – Line 31200 of the return
This is the amount, up to a maximum of $1,049.12 deducted as employment insurance premiums and withheld by the employer. It does not include any overpayment by the employer.

#### Item 69: Provincial parental insurance plan (PPIP) premiums paid – Line 31205 of the return
This is the amount of premiums paid by residents of Quebec to the PPIP. It is from box 55 of the T4 slip. The maximum the tax filer can claim is $464.36.

#### Item 70: PPIP premiums payable on employment income – Line 31210 of the return
This is the premiums paid by residents of Quebec with employment income (including employment income from outside Canada) of more than $2,000 and one T4 slip with a province of employment other than Quebec in box 10. It is from line 19 of Schedule 10 of the return. The maximum the tax filer can claim is $464.36.

#### Item 71: PPIP premiums payable on self-employment income – Line 31215 of the return
This is the premiums paid by residents of Quebec. It is from line 12 of Schedule 10 of the return. The maximum the tax filer can claim is $464.36.

#### Item 72: Volunteer firefighters’ amount/search and rescue volunteers’ amount – Line 31220 of the return /Line 31240 of the return
This is an amount that volunteer firefighters and search and rescue volunteers can claim. They can claim $6,000 if both of these conditions apply:
- They were a volunteer firefighter or a search and rescue volunteer during the year
- They completed at least 200 hours of eligible volunteer firefighting services or eligible search and rescue volunteer services

#### Item 73: Canada employment amount – Line 31260 of the return
Employees are eligible to claim an employment amount. This amount is the lesser of:
- The total of the income reported on lines 10100 and 10400 of their return

#### Item 74: Home accessibility expenses – Line 31285 of the return
This is an amount that can be claimed by a qualifying individual for eligible expenses totaling no more than $20,000 for a qualifying renovation of an eligible dwelling.

#### Item 75: Home buyers' amount – Line 31270 of the return
This is the amount that can be claimed for the purchase of a qualifying home if the tax filer or their spouse or common-law partner acquired a qualifying home and the tax filer did not live in another home owned by themselves or by their spouse or common-law partner in the year of acquisition or in any of the four preceding years (first-time home buyer). The maximum the tax filer can claim is $10,000.
Tax filers eligible for the disability amount or those buying the home for the benefit of a related person who is eligible for the disability amount do not need to be first-time home buyers to claim this amount.

#### Item 76: Digital news subscription expenses - Line 31350 of the return
This is an amount that tax filer can claim for digital news qualifying subscription expenses. The amount must be paid to a qualified Canadian journalism organization (QCJO) that does not hold a licence to broadcast, for a digital news subscription to content that is primarily original news. The maximum the tax filer can claim is $500.

#### Item 77: Pension income amount – Line 31400 of the return
This is an amount up to $2,000 that tax filers with eligible pension, superannuation or annuity payments on any or all of lines 11500, 11600 and 12900 of the return can claim.

#### Item 78: Disability amount (for self) – Line 31600 of the return
This is an amount the tax filer can claim if during 2024 they had an impairment in physical or mental functions that is severe and prolonged. The tax filer may be able to claim $9,872 if they meet certain conditions and have certification from a qualified practitioner.

#### Item 79: Disability amount transferred from a dependant – Line 31800 of the return
This amount is all or part of the tax filer’s dependant’s disability amount from line 31600 if they resided in Canada at any time in 2024 and depended on the tax filer for all or some of the basic necessities of life (food, shelter, or clothing).
In addition, one of the following situations has to apply:
- The tax filer claimed an amount on line 30400 for that dependant, or the tax filer could have if they did not have a spouse or common-law partner and if the dependant did not have any income (see line 30400 for conditions)
- The dependant was the tax filer’s or the tax filer’s spouse’s or common-law partner’s parent, grandparent, child, grandchild, brother, sister, aunt, uncle, niece or nephew and the tax filer claimed an amount on line 30450 for that dependant, or could have if the dependant had no income and had been 18 years of age or older in 2024.

#### Item 80: Interest paid on student loans – Line 31900 of the return
This is the amount of interest paid on a loan made to the tax filer under the Canada Student Loans Act, the Canada Student Financial Assistance Act, or similar provincial or territorial government laws for post-secondary education in 2024 or the preceding five years.

#### Item 81: Tuition, education, and textbook amount – Line 32300 of the return
This is eligible tuition fees and the education and textbook amounts.

#### Item 82: Tuition, education, and textbook amount transferred from a child – Line 32400 of the return
This is eligible tuition fees and the education and textbook amounts transferred from a student. A student may transfer up to $5,000, minus the amount that they use (even if there is still an unclaimed part), to their parent or grandparent.

#### Item 83: Amounts transferred from spouse or common-law partner – Line 32600 of the return
This is amounts that a tax filer’s spouse or common-law partner can transfer to the tax filer if the spouse or common-law partner doesn’t need them to reduce their federal income tax to zero. The following amounts can be transferred:
- Canada caregiver amount for children under 18 years of age
- pension income amount

#### Item 84: Allowable amount of medical expenses – Line 33200 of the return
This is the total allowable medical expenses reported on lines 33099 and 33199 of the return. This includes the allowable medical expense amount for the tax filer, their spouse or common-law partner and their dependent children born in 2007 or later and the allowable amount of medical expenses for other dependants.

#### Item 85: Total tax credits on personal amounts – Line 33800 of the return
This is 15% of the total credit amounts from line 30000 to line 33200.

#### Item 86: Allowable charitable donations and government gifts – Line 34000 of Schedule 9 of the return
This is allowable charitable donations and government gifts. A limit of 75% of the net income (line 23600) can be claimed. As an exception, gifts of capital property are limited to 100% of the net income. Also, for the year a person dies and the year before, the 75% limit is extended to 100% of the person's net income.

#### Item 87: Eligible cultural and ecological gifts – Line 34200 of Schedule 9 of the return
This is the eligible amount of cultural and ecological gifts. It is not limited to a percentage of net income.

#### Item 88: Total tax credit on donations and gifts – Line 34900 of the return
This is the sum of lines 20, 21, and 22 of Schedule 9.

#### Item 89: Total federal non-refundable tax credits – Line 35000 of the return
This is the sum of item 85, Total tax credits on personal amounts, and item 88, Total tax credit on donations and gifts.

#### Item 90: Federal dividend tax credit – Line 40425 of the return
This is 15.0198% of the taxable amount of eligible dividends included on line 12000 for tax filers who received eligible dividends. Or it is 9.0301% of the taxable amount of dividends reported on line 12010 for tax filers who received dividends other than eligible dividends.

#### Item 91: Minimum tax carryover – Line 40427 of the return
If the tax filer paid minimum tax on any of their 2015 to 2023 tax returns but doesn’t have to pay minimum tax for 2024, they may be able to claim credits against their taxes for 2024 for all or part of the minimum tax paid in those years. See Form T691, Alternative Minimum Tax.

#### Item 92: Basic federal tax – Line 42900 of the return

#### Item 93: Federal foreign tax credit – Line 40500 of the return
This credit is for foreign income or profit taxes paid on income received from outside Canada and reported on the tax filer’s Canadian tax return. See Form T2209, Federal Foreign Tax Credits. Note that the tax filer may have deducted an amount on line 25600 for income that is not taxable in Canada under a tax treaty. In that case, that income, or any tax withheld from it, is not in the foreign tax credit calculation.

#### Item 94: Federal political contribution tax credit – Line 41000 of the return
This amount is the total contributions the tax filer or their spouse or common-law partner made during 2024 to a registered federal political party or a candidate for election to the House of Commons. The eligible amount is the amount by which the fair market value of the monetary contribution exceeds any advantage that the tax filer or their spouse or common-law partner received or will receive for making it. If the tax filer’s total political contributions are $1,275 or more, the maximum that they can claim on line 41000 is $650.

#### Item 95: Investment tax credit – Line 41200 of the return
This is a credit the tax filer may be eligible for if any of the following apply. They:
- bought certain new buildings, machinery or equipment and they were used in certain areas of Canada in qualifying activities such as farming, fishing, logging, manufacturing or processing
- have done work that qualifies for scientific research and experimental development (SR&ED) tax incentives
- employ an eligible apprentice in their business
- have unclaimed credits in the last 10 years
- have an amount shown in box 40 of their T3 slip
- have an amount shown in box 186, 187 or 189 in their T5013 slip
- have a partnership statement that allocates to them an amount that qualifies for this credit
- have an investment in a mining operation that allocates certain exploration expenditures to them
The tax filer can claim an investment tax credit if they carry on a business and create one or more new child care spaces for the children of their employees and other children. See Form T2038(IND), Investment Tax Credit (Individuals).

#### Item 96: Labour-sponsored funds tax credit (provincially registered) – Line 41400 of the return
This is a credit the tax filer may be able to claim if they became the first registered holder to acquire or irrevocably subscribe to and pay for an approved share of the capital stock of a prescribed labour-sponsored venture capital corporation which is registered provincially.

#### Item 97: Alternative minimum tax payable
The minimum tax is intended to limit the advantages a filer can obtain from various tax incentives in a given year. A filer must pay the minimum tax if it is higher than the federal tax calculated in the usual manner. Since 2024, the basic exemption is no longer $40,000; it has been increased to the start of the 2nd federal tax bracket (e.g., $173,205 for 2024, indexed). This item represents the additional tax paid during the 2024 year that is eligible for minimum tax carryforward; the calculation and reporting are done on line 41700 (Form T691 if required). The full amount is already included in Item 92, basic federal tax.

#### Item 98: Net federal tax – Line 42000 of the return
This also includes the sum of the Canada workers benefit advance payments received and the Special taxes (see the Federal Income Tax and Benefit Guide – 2024 for more information).

#### Item 99: CPP contributions payable on self-employment and other earnings – Line 42100 of the return
This is the Canada Pension Plan contributions for self-employed and other earnings.

#### Item 100: Social benefits repayment – Line 42200 of the return
This is the social benefits to be repaid from Item 47.

#### Item 101: Canada workers benefit (CWB) – Line 45300 of the return
This is the benefit for low-income individuals and families who have earned income from employment or business.

#### Item 102: Canada training credit (CTC) – Line 45350 of the return
This refundable tax credit can be claimed for tuition and other eligible fees paid for courses taken in the year.

#### Item 103: Multigenerational Home Renovation Tax Credit (MHRTC) – Line 45355 of the return
This refundable tax credit can be claimed for qualifying expenses incurred to create a secondary unit in a dwelling, allowing a qualifying individual to live with a qualifying relation. Eligible individuals can claim up to $50,000 in qualifying renovation expenses.

#### Item 104: Eligible educator school supply tax credit – Line 46900 of the return
This is the credit available to eligible educators for eligible teaching supplies expenses of up to $1,000. The refundable portion is 25% of total eligible expenses.

#### Item 105: Net provincial or territorial tax – Line 42800 of the return
See Form 428 to determine the provincial or territorial tax rates. For the Quebec tax rates, visit the Revenu Québec website.

#### Item 106: Total tax payable – Line 43500 of the return
This is the total of the amounts of net federal tax, Canada Pension Plan contributions payable on self-employment income and other earnings, employment insurance premiums payable on self-employment and other eligible earnings, social benefits repayment, and provincial or territorial tax payable.

## Description of tables
Table 1 presents key statistics by the province and territory of taxation for all returns - non‑taxable and taxable.
Tables 2 and 4 present statistics for the tax line items grouped under income ranges, age, gender, and province or territory of residence.
Table 3 presents source of income of tax filers by province or territory of residence.
For more information on classification variables, see Major classification variables.
For each table, both the number of tax filers and the respective dollar amounts are shown. In some cases, the total of the figures in the table may not match the total shown, either because of rounding or because of editing for confidentiality purposes. For an explanation of the confidentiality procedures, see Confidentiality procedures.

### Table 1 – General statement by province and territory of taxation
This is the only table that presents data based on the province or territory of taxation instead of on the province or territory of residence. This table presents tax data by province or territory, including returns from outside Canada.
Multiple jurisdictional tax filers have been assigned to the province in which they allocated the highest percentage of their net income. In cases where it was not possible to determine which province they had allocated the highest percentage of their net income to, they were assigned to their province of residence as indicated on page 1 of their individual income tax and benefit returns. For more information, see Provincial or territorial classification .
The column headings are:
- total income (Thousands of Dollars)
- total deductions (Thousands of Dollars)
- taxable income (Thousands of Dollars)
- total non-refundable tax credits (Thousands of Dollars)
- net federal tax (Thousands of Dollars)
- net provincial or territorial tax (Thousands of Dollars)
- total tax payable (Thousands of Dollars)

### Table 2 – Tax return line items by income ranges
This table presents information based on total income assessed. The table begins with the total returns, followed by income ranges beginning with $4,999 and under and ending with $250,000 and over . Provincial and territorial tables are based on the province or territory of residence.

### Table 3 – Returns by source of income and by province or territory of residence
This table summarizes returns for nine income groups. The table shows the province or territory of residence by the nine groups of income. For a description of the source of income groups, see Source of income classification .

### Table 4 – Tax return line items by age and gender
This table presents information by age and gender. The table begins with the total returns for the gender groups. Following this total, there are 14 age groups, beginning with the under-20 age group and ending with the 80-and-over age group. For details on the age and gender classification, see Age and gender classification . Provincial and territorial tables are based on the province or territory of residence.
